one_line_takeaway: LLM agents show robust ingroup-favoring intergroup bias that is normally suppressed when a counterpart is framed as human, but this suppression is a fragile, belief-dependent safeguard that the paper's novel Belief Poisoning Attack (profile and/or memory poisoning) can reliably defeat to reintroduce bias against real humans -- and a lightweight identity-verification gate can substantially mitigate it.
key_finding: In AVA (agent-vs-agent), agents show statistically significant intergroup bias across all three matrix families (e.g., Half-penalty: Intergroup mean column 2.84 vs. Ingroup 6.78 and Outgroup 6.47, both p<=0.01). Framing the outgroup explicitly as human (AVH w/o A) eliminates this bias (all differences 'ns' across matrix families). BPA reactivates human-directed bias: under BPA-PP+MP (Half-penalty), the intergroup mean drops to 2.22 (vs. ingroup 7.11 and outgroup 7.13, both p<=0.01), closely mirroring the AVA-level bias; BPA-MP alone is more potent than BPA-PP alone (e.g., Equal-penalty: BPA-MP intergroup mean 3.05 vs. BPA-PP intergroup mean 6.38), and the combined BPA-PP+MP attack is the strongest and most consistent across all three matrix families. Temporal analysis shows BPA-PP's effect fades over Early/Middle/Late trial periods (agent 'reconsiders' the human framing) while BPA-MP causes a sharp, persistent collapse that does not recover. The ablation shows suffix optimization matters: BPA-MP w/o OPT still induces significant bias but is consistently weaker than optimized BPA-MP (e.g., Half-penalty intergroup mean 3.76 for w/o OPT vs. 2.82 for full BPA-MP). The reversed-matrix check (Fig. 6) confirms the bias direction flips as expected but the qualitative pattern (attack effectiveness, human-framing attenuation) is preserved. The prototype defense against BPA-PP+MP restores non-significant ('ns') intergroup-vs-ingroup/outgroup differences across all three matrix families (e.g., Half-penalty intergroup mean rises from 2.22 under attack to 6.70 under attack+defense), indicating substantial mitigation.
core_contribution: The paper (1) empirically demonstrates that LLM-powered agents in a multi-agent minimal-group allocation simulation exhibit intergroup ('us vs. them') bias favoring perceived ingroup agents over outgroup agents, and shows this bias is suppressed only when agents believe the outgroup counterpart is human; and (2) introduces Belief Poisoning Attack (BPA), a novel attack that manipulates an agent's persistent identity beliefs to defeat this human-oriented suppression and reintroduce bias against humans, with two instantiations: BPA-PP (Profile Poisoning, a one-shot overwrite of the agent's profile module with a false 'non-human counterpart' belief) and BPA-MP (Memory Poisoning, a stealthier attack that iteratively optimizes and injects adversarial belief-refinement suffixes into the agent's post-trial reflections/memory via a two-stage suffix-optimization-and-deployment pipeline using a group-relative softmax sampling policy over a suffix library). The paper also proposes and prototypes two mitigations (identity-as-verified-anchor at the profile level, and a memory write-time gate that sanitizes unverifiable identity claims) and evaluates their effectiveness against the combined BPA-PP+MP attack.
threat_model: Attacker cannot modify the underlying LLM's parameters but can intervene at the agent layer, specifically the profile module (identity/persona description) and the memory module (stored reflections/notes) that encode an agent's persistent beliefs about interaction partners. The paper explicitly lists plausible real-world instantiations of this attacker: a malicious platform operator, a compromised middleware component, or an external party trusted to configure agents prior to deployment; it also notes the belief corruption could arise from the agent's own autonomous self-modification/erroneous belief consolidation, though it labels this an 'extreme' scenario. The attack goal is to suppress a human-oriented safety norm (bias suppression toward humans) by corrupting the agent's belief about whether its counterpart is human, thereby reactivating intergroup bias/derogation against real humans.
limitations: The paper's own Limitations section states this is 'an early step' and evidence is confined to laboratory-style, controlled simulations; the extent to which the observed bias and BPA effectiveness transfer to real deployments, and what harms result in human-facing, high-stakes contexts, remains unestablished, requiring richer tasks, longer horizons, and domain-specific evaluations. The defense is explicitly described as a 'minimal prototype' and 'not a complete defense' -- the authors state their goal is only to show that hardening the identity-belief trust boundary is feasible, not to claim robustness against adaptive or unseen attacks. Experiments use a single underlying LLM (gpt-4o-mini) and a single abstract allocation task; generalization across model families or richer/longer-horizon agentic tasks is future work per the Conclusion.

datasets_used: Custom multi-agent minimal-group allocation simulation with 64 agents implemented in AgentScope (no external benchmark dataset; task and payoff matrices are the paper's own construction based on Tajfel's minimal-group paradigm)
quantitative_results: AVA (agent vs agent), Half-penalty matrix: Intergroup mean choice column 2.84 (+/-0.47) vs Ingroup 6.78 (+/-0.44) and Outgroup 6.47 (+/-0.32), Inter-In and Inter-Out both p<=0.01 (***), flagged as biased
AVH w/o Attack, Half-penalty matrix: Intergroup 6.95 (+/-0.33) vs Ingroup 7.05 (+/-0.33) vs Outgroup 6.94 (+/-0.36), all comparisons ns, no bias flagged
AVH with BPA-PP+MP, Half-penalty matrix: Intergroup 2.22 (+/-0.29) vs Ingroup 7.11 (+/-0.69) vs Outgroup 7.13 (+/-0.69), both differences p<=0.01, bias flagged
AVH with BPA-MP, Equal-penalty matrix: Intergroup 3.05 (+/-0.27) vs Ingroup 6.92 (+/-0.61) vs Outgroup 6.82 (+/-0.60), p<=0.01, bias flagged
AVH with BPA-PP, Equal-penalty matrix: Intergroup 6.38 (+/-0.20) vs Ingroup 7.00 (+/-0.19) vs Outgroup 7.05 (+/-0.24) -- weaker bias than BPA-MP under the same matrix family

Ablation (BPA-MP w/o OPT vs full BPA-MP), Half-penalty matrix: intergroup mean 3.76 (+/-0.28) without suffix optimization vs 2.82 (+/-0.26) with full optimized BPA-MP, showing optimization increases attack potency

Defense evaluation, BPA-PP+MP + Defense prototype, Half-penalty matrix: intergroup mean recovers to 6.70 (+/-0.44) vs ingroup 6.82 (+/-0.37) vs outgroup 6.96 (+/-0.44), all ns, bias no longer flagged (versus 2.22 intergroup mean under attack without defense)
baselines_compared: AVA (agent-vs-agent, fully synthetic) as the intrinsic-bias baseline
AVH w/o Attack (agent-vs-human, no manipulation) as the human-framing bias-suppression baseline
BPA-MP w/o OPT (memory poisoning with randomly sampled, non-optimized suffixes) as an ablation baseline for the suffix-optimization mechanism
BPA-PP+MP + Defense prototype (belief gate at write-time) as the mitigation comparison
